**Sha Tin**, also spelt **Shatin**, is a neighbourhood along [Shing Mun River](/wiki/Shing_Mun_River "Shing Mun River") in the eastern [New Territories](/wiki/New_Territories "New Territories"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"). Administratively, it is part of the [Sha Tin District](/wiki/Sha_Tin_District "Sha Tin District"). Sha Tin is one of the neighbourhoods of the [Sha Tin New Town](/wiki/Sha_Tin_New_Town "Sha Tin New Town") project.

The new town was founded in 1973 under the [New Towns Development Programme](/wiki/New_towns_of_Hong_Kong "New towns of Hong Kong") of the Hong Kong government. Its current name was named after the nearby village of [Sha Tin Wai](/wiki/Sha_Tin_Wai "Sha Tin Wai"). The literal English translation is 'Sand Fields'.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sha_Tin&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[Tai Wai Village](/wiki/Tai_Wai_Village "Tai Wai Village"), located in [Tai Wai](/wiki/Tai_Wai "Tai Wai"), next to Sha Tin, and the oldest and largest [walled village](/wiki/Walled_villages_of_Hong_Kong "Walled villages of Hong Kong") in [Sha Tin District](/wiki/Sha_Tin_District "Sha Tin District"), was built in 1574, during the [Ming Dynasty](/wiki/Ming_Dynasty "Ming Dynasty").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Before [British rule in Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong"), the area of Sha Tin and its vicinity was referred to as **Lek Yuen** (瀝源, 沥源, lit. "source of trickling" or "source of clear water"). In 1899, when colonial surveyors George P Tate and his assistant William John Newland were dispatched to survey the New Territories, they likely mistook the name of the [Sha Tin Wai](/wiki/Sha_Tin_Wai "Sha Tin Wai") village as the name of the area and hence "Sha Tin" has been used ever since.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) Nowadays, the original name is used to refer to [Lek Yuen Estate](/wiki/Lek_Yuen_Estate "Lek Yuen Estate").

Sha Tin was the location of [the first flight](/wiki/Aviation_history_of_Hong_Kong "Aviation history of Hong Kong") of a powered aircraft in Hong Kong in 1911. The aeroplane, a Farman Mk II bi-plane named “Wanda”, was flown by Belgian pilot Charles Van den Born.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) The plane was later named as the *Spirit of Sha Tin* (沙田精神號). A full size replica of this plane now hangs in [Hong Kong International Airport](/wiki/Hong_Kong_International_Airport "Hong Kong International Airport").

The area was formerly agricultural farmland. Before Sha Tin's development into a [new town](/wiki/New_towns_of_Hong_Kong "New towns of Hong Kong"), [Hung Mui Kuk](/wiki/Hung_Mui_Kuk "Hung Mui Kuk") (紅梅谷), southwest of Sha Tin, was perennially the main site for school [picnics](/wiki/Picnic "Picnic"). The hillside area remains a popular barbecue site.

In 1956, a [market township](/wiki/Market_town "Market town"): **Sha Tin Hui**, was established on top of unused agricultural fields at the present location of Sha Tin Centre Street and [New Town Plaza](/wiki/New_Town_Plaza "New Town Plaza") shopping centre, next to the [Sha Tin station](/wiki/Sha_Tin_station "Sha Tin station") of the [MTR](/wiki/MTR "MTR") [East Rail line](/wiki/East_Rail_line "East Rail line"). The township was founded by Den Lau (劉贊瑞), the son of businessman Lau Hey Shing (劉希成) who owned the 150,000 sq ft. plot of land. Sha Tin Hui contained five streets with shops, restaurants, and residential buildings. Modern facilities such as a post office and a cinema were also put in place.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In 1962 Sha Tin Hui was severely damaged by [typhoon Wanda](/wiki/Typhoon_Wanda_(1962) "Typhoon Wanda (1962)"), and subsequently had to be rebuilt. In 1979 Sha Tin Hui was demolished as a result of the government's expansion plans. The land was leased to [Sun Hung Kai properties](/wiki/Sun_Hung_Kai_Properties "Sun Hung Kai Properties"), who then developed the area into [New Town Plaza](/wiki/New_Town_Plaza "New Town Plaza") shopping centre.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

Starting in the 1970s, the area became part of the [Sha Tin New Town](/wiki/Sha_Tin_New_Town "Sha Tin New Town") development. Since then, the economy in the area has greatly improved and living standards have also increased. Sha Tin Town Centre was developed during the mid-1980s to help "link the town's currently dispersed residents into one cohesive community."[[7]](#cite_note-7) The 18-hectare site, adjacent to the railway station, was built up in stages to house an array of uses including the [New Town Plaza](/wiki/New_Town_Plaza "New Town Plaza"), numerous smaller shopping malls, [Sha Tin Park](/wiki/Sha_Tin_Park "Sha Tin Park"), a magistracy, [library](/wiki/Sha_Tin_Public_Library "Sha Tin Public Library"), [town hall](/wiki/Sha_Tin_Town_Hall "Sha Tin Town Hall"), marriage registry, hotel, town square, and several residential towers.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sha_Tin&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/ShaTin-ShingMunRiver-EarlyStageOfDevelopment.jpg/250px-ShaTin-ShingMunRiver-EarlyStageOfDevelopment.jpg)](/wiki/File:ShaTin-ShingMunRiver-EarlyStageOfDevelopment.jpg)

[Sha Tin New Town](/wiki/Sha_Tin_New_Town "Sha Tin New Town") under development in the late 1970s.

Sha Tin is located in a valley, on both sides of the [Shing Mun River](/wiki/Shing_Mun_River "Shing Mun River"), running from the southwest to the northeast. It is bordered by [Tai Wai](/wiki/Tai_Wai "Tai Wai") in the southwest and by [Fo Tan](/wiki/Fo_Tan "Fo Tan") (left bank) and [Shek Mun](/wiki/Shek_Mun "Shek Mun") (right bank) in the northeast.

### Cross-border activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sha_Tin&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Cross-border activities")]

Due to their proximity to the Shenzhen border, towns in the northern parts of Hong Kong, notably Sheung Shui and Yuen Long, have become hubs for parallel traders who have been buying up large quantities of goods, forcing up local prices and disrupting the daily lives of local citizens.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) Since 2012, there has been an increase in mainland [parallel traders](/wiki/Parallel_trading_in_Hong_Kong "Parallel trading in Hong Kong") arriving in the [North District](/wiki/North_District,_Hong_Kong "North District, Hong Kong") of Hong Kong to re-export infant formula and household products – goods popular with mainlanders – across the border to Shenzhen.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The volume of smuggling activity spilled over into Tuen Mun and Sha Tin in 2014.

The first anti-parallel trading protest was started at Sheung Shui in September 2012.[[11]](#cite_note-11) As government efforts to limit the adverse impact of mainland trafficking were widely seen as inadequate, so there have been further [subsequent protests](/wiki/Anti-parallel_trading_protests "Anti-parallel trading protests") in towns in the New Territories including Sha Tin.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-20150309bloomberg-13)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sha_Tin&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Architecture")]

## Culture, sports and recreational facilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sha_Tin&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Culture, sports and recreational facilities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/JG_%26_HKJC.JPG/250px-JG_%26_HKJC.JPG)](/wiki/File:JG_%26_HKJC.JPG)

Sha Tin racecourse.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Hong_Kong_Heritage_Museum_%28staff_entrance%29.JPG/250px-Hong_Kong_Heritage_Museum_%28staff_entrance%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hong_Kong_Heritage_Museum_(staff_entrance).JPG)

[Hong Kong Heritage Museum](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Heritage_Museum "Hong Kong Heritage Museum").

There are numerous cultural, recreational and sport facilities in Sha Tin including the [Town Hall](/wiki/Sha_Tin_Town_Hall "Sha Tin Town Hall"), swimming pools, football pitches, indoor recreation centres and various track and field facilities for the use of Sha Tin residents.

The 8-hectare [Sha Tin Park](/wiki/Sha_Tin_Park "Sha Tin Park") was opened to public in 1988. Apart from its horticultural gardens and impressive water features, it also includes a large open plaza and a bandstand. The Ma On Shan Park, which is adjacent to Ma On Shan Swimming Pool, occupies 5.5 hectare of land.

The [Sha Tin Racecourse](/wiki/Sha_Tin_Racecourse "Sha Tin Racecourse"), occupying approximately 70 hectares, rests on reclaimed flatland. At the centre of the racecourse is the Penfold Garden which opens to the public on non-racing days.

Located in [Tai Wai](/wiki/Tai_Wai "Tai Wai"), the [Hong Kong Heritage Museum](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Heritage_Museum "Hong Kong Heritage Museum") was opened at the end of 2000. Apart from introducing the art, culture and history of the New Territories, the museum also exhibits a variety of cultural artifacts for public appreciation. It has pop culture exhibitions about [Bruce Lee](/wiki/Bruce_Lee "Bruce Lee"), [Cantonese Opera](/wiki/Cantonese_Opera "Cantonese Opera"), [Jin Yong](/wiki/Jin_Yong "Jin Yong"), development of Hong Kong popular music, film, and television and radio programmes. The museum, which can accommodate 6,000 visitors, is the largest in the territory.

Cycling has been a distinctive feature in Sha Tin and is very popular among both local people and visitors. The first [cycle track](/wiki/Cycle_track "Cycle track") in Sha Tin was opened to public in 1981, running along [Tolo Highway](/wiki/Tolo_Highway "Tolo Highway") to [Tai Po](/wiki/Tai_Po "Tai Po"), and this remains the territory's most popular cycling venue, drawing many occasional riders at the weekends, as well as dedicated cyclists. To tie in with the development of Ma On Shan, the cycle track was extended to [Ma On Shan](/wiki/Ma_On_Shan_(town) "Ma On Shan (town)").

Hiking is also a popular activity around Sha Tin. There are several starting points including [Hin Tin](/wiki/Hin_Tin "Hin Tin") Village, [Sha Tin Tau](/wiki/Sha_Tin_Tau "Sha Tin Tau") Village and [Hung Mui Kuk](/wiki/Hung_Mui_Kuk "Hung Mui Kuk") Barbecue Area leading to the track of Lion Rock Mountain hiking route. It takes 1 hour to 4 hours to complete the track depending on the chosen starting point and ending point.

A [Dragon Boat](/wiki/Dragon_Boat "Dragon Boat") Racing competition is taking annually on [Shing Mun River](/wiki/Shing_Mun_River "Shing Mun River") since 1984, at the time of the [Dragon Boat Festival](/wiki/Dragon_Boat_Festival "Dragon Boat Festival").[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Local delicacies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sha_Tin&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Local delicacies")]

Sha Tin is famous for certain local variants of [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese_cuisine "Cantonese cuisine") [food](/wiki/Chinese_cuisine "Chinese cuisine") such as *ShanSui [Tofu](/wiki/Tofu "Tofu")* (山水豆腐; 'mountain-water beancurd'), barbecued pigeon and [chicken congee](/wiki/Chicken_congee "Chicken congee"). The cooked food stalls in [Wo Che Estate](/wiki/Wo_Che_Estate "Wo Che Estate") and [Fo Tan](/wiki/Fo_Tan "Fo Tan") are hotspots for food.

### Roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sha_Tin&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Roads")]

The road network in Sha Tin is well developed to provide efficient cross-town and local access traffic. Connection between Sha Tin and Kowloon mainly relies on the [Lion Rock Tunnel](/wiki/Lion_Rock_Tunnel "Lion Rock Tunnel"), [Tate's Cairn Tunnel](/wiki/Tate%27s_Cairn_Tunnel "Tate's Cairn Tunnel"), [Shing Mun Tunnel](/wiki/Shing_Mun_Tunnel "Shing Mun Tunnel") and [Tai Po Road](/wiki/Tai_Po_Road "Tai Po Road") which makes it easy to reach from many areas of Kowloon as well as from Tsuen Wan.

* Tai Po Road spans from [Sham Shui Po](/wiki/Sham_Shui_Po "Sham Shui Po") in [Kowloon](/wiki/Kowloon "Kowloon") to [Tai Po](/wiki/Tai_Po "Tai Po") in the [New Territories](/wiki/New_Territories "New Territories"), connecting Sha Tin en route. It was once the only road connecting Kowloon and the eastern part of the New Territories.
* Opened in the 1960s, the Lion Rock Tunnel offers access to Central Kowloon.
* The [Tate's Cairn Highway](/wiki/Tate%27s_Cairn_Highway "Tate's Cairn Highway") was completed in 1991, connecting East Kowloon ([Wong Tai Sin](/wiki/Wong_Tai_Sin,_Hong_Kong "Wong Tai Sin, Hong Kong"), [Diamond Hill](/wiki/Diamond_Hill "Diamond Hill"), [Choi Hung](/wiki/Choi_Hung "Choi Hung"), [Kowloon Bay](/wiki/Kowloon_Bay "Kowloon Bay"), and [Kwun Tong](/wiki/Kwun_Tong "Kwun Tong")) and Hong Kong Island via the [Eastern Harbour Crossing](/wiki/Eastern_Harbour_Crossing "Eastern Harbour Crossing") with the North East New Territories (Sha Tin, Tai Po and [Fanling](/wiki/Fanling "Fanling")) via the Tolo Highway and through Tate's Cairn Tunnel.
* Route connects Sha Tin to [Tsuen Wan](/wiki/Tsuen_Wan "Tsuen Wan") via the [Shing Mun Tunnels](/wiki/Shing_Mun_Tunnels "Shing Mun Tunnels") and to [Hong Kong Island](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Island "Hong Kong Island") via the [Western Harbour Crossing](/wiki/Western_Harbour_Crossing "Western Harbour Crossing"). The travelling time is only about 30 minutes.
* [Sai Sha Road](/wiki/Sai_Sha_Road "Sai Sha Road") was opened in 1988, connecting Sha Tin to [Sai Kung](/wiki/Sai_Kung_Town "Sai Kung Town") via [Ma On Shan](/wiki/Ma_On_Shan_(town) "Ma On Shan (town)"). Route T7, opened in August 2004, allows traffic to bypass Ma On Shan Town Centre when going from north of Ma On Shan to Sai Kung.
* An expressway (via Eagle's Nest Tunnel) connecting [Cheung Sha Wan](/wiki/Cheung_Sha_Wan "Cheung Sha Wan") in Kowloon to Sha Tin was opened on 21 March 2008. It aims at distributing traffic from Sha Tin and the area to its north, to [Lai Chi Kok](/wiki/Lai_Chi_Kok "Lai Chi Kok"), [Kwai Chung](/wiki/Kwai_Chung "Kwai Chung"), and the airport at [Chek Lap Kok](/wiki/Chek_Lap_Kok "Chek Lap Kok") and [Mong Kok](/wiki/Mong_Kok "Mong Kok"). It has shortened the trip from Sha Tin to the [Chek Lap Kok International Airport](/wiki/Hong_Kong_International_Airport "Hong Kong International Airport") to around 40 minutes.

At present, there are over 110 routes of public bus serving Sha Tin.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Railway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sha_Tin&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Railway")]

* The [MTR](/wiki/MTR "MTR") ([East Rail line](/wiki/East_Rail_line "East Rail line")) is a major means of transportation between [Admiralty](/wiki/Admiralty,_Hong_Kong "Admiralty, Hong Kong") and [Lo Wu](/wiki/Lo_Wu "Lo Wu") via Sha Tin. After the electrification of the line between 1979 and 1983, the East Rail now carries over 730,000 passengers daily.
* The [Tuen Ma line](/wiki/Tuen_Ma_line "Tuen Ma line") opened on 21 December 2004. The 56.4 km long railway has 27 stations linking [West Rail line](/wiki/West_Rail_line "West Rail line") at [Hung Hom](/wiki/Hung_Hom "Hung Hom") via [Kai Tak](/wiki/Kai_Tak "Kai Tak"). The MTR Maintenance Centre is located in Tai Wai.
